title: Cybersecurity Basics in Education Technology
description: Learn essential cybersecurity practices protecting educational technology systems, student data, and institutional information. Explore three key domains: authentication, data protection, and threat recognition.

nodes.title: Why Cybersecurity Matters in Education Technology
nodes.content: Cybersecurity is critical in education technology because educational institutions handle some of the most sensitive personal data: student grades, enrollment records, health information, and family contact details. Educational platforms like learning management systems (LMS), gradebooks, and student information systems (SIS) are frequent targets for attackers seeking this valuable data. A single breach can compromise thousands of students' privacy and institutional trust. Understanding cybersecurity helps you protect your institution's systems, colleague access, and most importantly, student data.

nodes.title: Choose Your Learning Path
nodes.content: Effective cybersecurity in education technology rests on three foundational pillars: verifying who users are and controlling their access, protecting and encrypting sensitive data, and recognizing threats and responding swiftly. Choose which area you'd like to explore first.

nodes.title: Authentication: Verifying Identity in EdTech
nodes.content: Authentication verifies that a user is who they claim to be. In education technology, strong authentication protects student accounts, teacher credentials, and administrative access from unauthorized users. Passwords alone are vulnerable if weak or reused. Multi-factor authentication (MFA) adds security layers: something you know (password), something you have (phone or security key), or something you are (biometric). Learning management systems should require MFA for administrative accounts and encourage it for all users to prevent account takeovers that could expose student rosters, grades, and personal information.

nodes.title: Authentication in Action: A Real Scenario
nodes.content: Maria, a middle school teacher, uses the same password for her Google Workspace account (connected to the school's Google Classroom LMS) and personal email. When a data breach exposes credentials from an unrelated website, a hacker tries that password on the school account. With MFA enabled, even with the correct password, the hacker cannot log in without Maria's phone to verify the second factor. Without MFA, the hacker would have accessed all her classes, student submissions, grades, and class rosters.

nodes.title: Data Protection & Privacy: Safeguarding Student Information
nodes.content: Data protection ensures that sensitive educational information—grades, transcripts, health records, family contact information, and learning analytics—remains confidential and accessible only to authorized personnel. Encryption scrambles data so only those with the correct decryption key can read it. Educational institutions must comply with regulations like FERPA (Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act) in the United States. Best practices include: encrypting data in transit (using HTTPS) and at rest (on servers), implementing role-based access control (so teachers see only their students' data), and maintaining audit logs to track who accessed what data and when.

nodes.title: Data Protection in Action: The Cost of Neglect
nodes.content: A school district's online gradebook platform is breached due to unencrypted storage and weak access controls. Attackers download grades, names, student ID numbers, and parent contact information for 50,000 students, then sell the data. The district faces lawsuits, regulatory fines, and loss of community trust. If the platform had encrypted all student records using strong encryption keys stored separately, the stolen data would be unreadable. If access controls had limited each teacher to only their students, the breach would have exposed far fewer students.

nodes.title: Threat Recognition & Response: Spotting Attacks
nodes.content: Threats in education technology include phishing (deceptive emails tricking users into revealing credentials), malware (malicious software), ransomware (software demanding payment to unlock files), and man-in-the-middle attacks (intercepting unencrypted communications). In educational settings, attackers use social engineering: phishing emails impersonate the IT help desk, principal, or financial aid office. Recognition is your first line of defense: look for suspicious sender addresses, unusual urgency, spelling errors, password requests (legitimate IT never asks via email), or suspicious attachments. Reporting suspected threats to your IT department and avoiding unknown links are critical responses.

nodes.title: Threat Recognition in Action: Phishing Awareness
nodes.content: University staff receive an email appearing to be from the IT help desk: "URGENT: Verify Your Portal Access Now." The email claims accounts will be disabled unless they verify by clicking a link and entering their campus ID and password. The link mimics the real login page but is controlled by attackers. Experienced staff recognize red flags: legitimate IT never requests passwords via email, the urgency is artificial, and the sender's domain is slightly misspelled. They report it to IT, which confirms the phishing, blocks the domain, and alerts everyone. Staff who fell for it report immediately so IT can reset their passwords before attackers exploit the credentials.

nodes.title: Bringing It Together: A Holistic Cybersecurity Approach
nodes.content: Effective cybersecurity in education technology integrates three pillars: (1) Authentication ensures only legitimate users access systems through strong passwords and MFA, preventing unauthorized account takeovers. (2) Data Protection encrypts sensitive information and restricts access through role-based controls, so breaches are limited and data remains confidential. (3) Threat Awareness empowers everyone to recognize and report attacks, stopping social engineering before it succeeds. These pillars work together: strong authentication prevents many attacks, encryption protects data despite breaches, and threat awareness stops threats in their tracks. Cybersecurity is a shared responsibility—every educator, administrator, and support staff member plays a role in protecting student privacy and institutional mission.

nodes.title: You're Now a Cybersecurity-Aware Educator
nodes.content: Congratulations! You've learned foundational cybersecurity practices protecting education technology systems and student data. Key takeaways: use strong unique passwords and enable multi-factor authentication on sensitive accounts; treat student data as confidential and report unauthorized access; report suspicious emails and links immediately to IT; and remember that every person in the educational community protects students, families, and institutional trust. Stay vigilant and continue learning as threats evolve.
